sourceTitle: The ultimate guide to Notion templates

options.text: A prebuilt Notion page that can be added to a workspace.
options.explanation: Correct – a template is a ready‑made page you duplicate into your workspace.

options.text: A third‑party app that integrates with Notion.
options.explanation: Wrong – templates are native Notion pages, not external apps.

options.text: A collection of Notion’s API endpoints.
options.explanation: Wrong – templates have nothing to do with the API.

options.text: A Notion‑specific programming language.
options.explanation: Wrong – Notion does not have its own programming language.

questions.question: Where can you access the Notion Template Gallery on the web?

options.text: notion.com/templates
options.explanation: Correct – the gallery is located at notion.com/templates.

options.text: notion.com/gallery
options.explanation: Wrong – that URL does not exist for templates.

options.text: notion.com/help/templates
options.explanation: Wrong – the help center URL is different; the gallery lives at /templates.

options.text: notion.com/marketplace
options.explanation: Wrong – Notion’s marketplace is for integrations, not for templates.

questions.question: If you are already signed into Notion, what happens when you click “Start with this template”?

options.text: The template is instantly duplicated into your Private section.
options.explanation: Correct – signed‑in users get the template added directly to their workspace.

options.text: You are prompted to create a new Notion account.
options.explanation: Wrong – you only need an account if you’re not signed in.

options.text: The template is sent to your email as a PDF.
options.explanation: Wrong – templates are added as Notion pages, not emailed.

options.text: Notion opens a new browser tab with a read‑only view of the template.
options.explanation: Wrong – the template is added, not opened in read‑only mode.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT listed as a key characteristic of a good Notion template?

options.text: Useful – solves a clear problem for a defined audience.
options.explanation: Wrong – usefulness is a core characteristic.

options.text: Differentiated – visually distinct from a basic Word document.
options.explanation: Wrong – differentiation is emphasized in the guide.

options.text: Scalable – works well for many users and large amounts of content.
options.explanation: Wrong – scalability is a recommended quality.

options.text: Visually identical to a standard Word document.
options.explanation: Correct – a good template should NOT simply mimic a Word doc; it should leverage Notion’s flexibility.

questions.question: When customizing a duplicated template, which element cannot be directly edited from the page view?

options.text: Page cover photo
options.explanation: Wrong – you can change the cover photo from the page settings.

options.text: Database property type
options.explanation: Wrong – property types can be edited within the database.

options.text: System‑level permissions for the workspace
options.explanation: Correct – permissions are managed at the workspace or page level, not directly from a duplicated template’s content view.

options.text: Block type (e.g., callout, toggle)
options.explanation: Wrong – you can add, remove, or change block types freely.

questions.question: To publish a Notion page as a shareable template, which toggle must be turned on?

options.text: Allow duplicate as template
options.explanation: Correct – this toggle enables others to duplicate the page as a template.

options.text: Make page public
options.explanation: Wrong – making the page public is required, but without the duplicate toggle it isn’t a template.

options.text: Enable comments
options.explanation: Wrong – comments are unrelated to template duplication.

options.text: Lock page
options.explanation: Wrong – locking would prevent editing, not sharing as a template.

questions.question: Which sequence of actions correctly describes how to submit a template to the Notion Template Gallery?

options.text: Visit notion.com/templates → click “Submit a template” → fill out the form with a public link, name, description, and category.
options.explanation: Correct – these are the exact steps outlined in the guide.

options.text: Email Notion support with your template file attached.
options.explanation: Wrong – submissions are done via the web form, not email.

options.text: Upload the template to the Notion App Store.
options.explanation: Wrong – there is no App Store for templates.

options.text: Post the template link on the Notion community forum and wait for approval.
options.explanation: Wrong – while community sharing is possible, official gallery submission uses the form.

questions.question: How does the Template Gallery help users locate relevant templates?

options.text: It provides a search bar, category navigation, popular templates, and featured creators.
options.explanation: Correct – the guide lists all these navigation aids.

options.text: It only shows templates curated by Notion staff.
options.explanation: Wrong – the gallery includes community‑created templates, not just staff picks.

options.text: It requires users to browse every category manually.
options.explanation: Wrong – the search bar and filters reduce manual browsing.

options.text: It displays templates based on the user’s geographic location.
options.explanation: Wrong – location isn’t a factor in template discovery.

questions.question: Why is scalability a crucial factor when designing a template for team use?

options.text: It ensures the template can accommodate many collaborators and large volumes of content without breaking.
options.explanation: Correct – scalability means the template remains functional as the team grows and data accumulates.

options.text: It guarantees the template will look identical on all devices.
options.explanation: Wrong – device compatibility is about responsiveness, not scalability.

options.text: It makes the template automatically generate AI‑powered insights.
options.explanation: Wrong – AI insights are unrelated to scalability.

options.text: It limits the number of users who can access the template to prevent overload.
options.explanation: Wrong – scalability is about supporting more users, not limiting them.

questions.question: Which action would most likely violate the Notion Template Gallery Guidelines?

options.text: Publishing a template that contains offensive language or discriminatory content.
options.explanation: Correct – the guidelines require templates to be inoffensive and properly spelled.

options.text: Including a short Loom video tutorial inside the template.
options.explanation: Wrong – providing instructions is encouraged, not prohibited.

options.text: Submitting one template at a time, even though you have many to share.
options.explanation: Wrong – the guide explicitly states you can submit multiple templates, just one per submission.

options.text: Using correct spelling and clear structure throughout the template.
options.explanation: Wrong – this aligns with the guidelines, not a violation.
